---
title: "Grub2 Config"
date: 2011-01-21
forum: New to Ubuntu

### Post by Jacobah on 2011-01-21
Hey I would like to edit the grub.cfg file to make it so that ubuntu doesn't show up twice and that the vista entry doesn't show up at all. It says don't edit this file, but I get the feeling that it just doesn't trust me to edit it without killing it. I read around and it seems that with the original grub.lst files you could put # in front of whatever you didn't want to show up, is that safe and will it work with grub2? Here is what it looks like: 
```
#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro   quiet splash
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    echo    'Loading Linux 2.6.35-24-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-22-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-22-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro   quiet splash
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-22-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-22-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    echo    'Loading Linux 2.6.35-22-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-22-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-22-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4eccdcc8ccdcac09
    chainloader +1
}
menuentry "Windows Vista (loader) (on /dev/sda3)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos3)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 6638be0438bdd373
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
```

EDIT: I just used the grub customizer and it worked like a charm.

### Post by sikander3786 on 2011-01-21
For what you intend to do, you need to make changes to /etc/default/grub and /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober and then run this command to regenerate grub.cfg.

```
sudo update-grub
```

But you'll not be able to boot Windows then. I think you know that...

---

> **sikander3786 said:**

```
sudo update-grub
```But you'll not be able to boot Windows then. I think you know that...
Are you saying I won't be able to boot windows because I'm hiding Vista? Because I don't even have vista installed on any drive, I think that's showing up because Vista was originally installed on the drive ubuntu is on now. I use Windows 7 on a completely different drive. I just wanna hide the 2nd ubuntu entry and the Vista entry.

### Post by joeborder on 2011-01-21
If I understand correctly your current grub includes older versions of Ubuntu that you don't use, obviously, and some other windows vista drive that holds no use, a backup drive perhaps? In which case all you would need to do is take the menu entries out.

Try replacing your grub.cfg with this.

```

#
# DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE
#
# It is automatically generated by grub-mkconfig using templates
# from /etc/grub.d and settings from /etc/default/grub
#

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/00_header ###
if [ -s $prefix/grubenv ]; then
  set have_grubenv=true
  load_env
fi
set default="0"
if [ "${prev_saved_entry}" ]; then
  set saved_entry="${prev_saved_entry}"
  save_env saved_entry
  set prev_saved_entry=
  save_env prev_saved_entry
  set boot_once=true
fi

function savedefault {
  if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then
    saved_entry="${chosen}"
    save_env saved_entry
  fi
}

function recordfail {
  set recordfail=1
  if [ -n "${have_grubenv}" ]; then if [ -z "${boot_once}" ]; then save_env recordfail; fi; fi
}

function load_video {
  insmod vbe
  insmod vga
}

insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
if loadfont /usr/share/grub/unicode.pf2 ; then
  set gfxmode=640x480
  load_video
  insmod gfxterm
fi
terminal_output gfxterm
insmod part_msdos
insmod ext2
set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
set locale_dir=($root)/boot/grub/locale
set lang=en
insmod gettext
if [ "${recordfail}" = 1 ]; then
  set timeout=-1
else
  set timeout=10
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/00_header ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###
set menu_color_normal=white/black
set menu_color_highlight=black/light-gray
### END /etc/grub.d/05_debian_theme ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro   quiet splash
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic
}
menuentry 'Ubuntu, with Linux 2.6.35-24-generic (recovery mode)' --class ubuntu --class gnu-linux --class gnu --class os {
    recordfail
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    echo    'Loading Linux 2.6.35-24-generic ...'
    linux    /boot/vmlinuz-2.6.35-24-generic root=UUID=896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995 ro single 
    echo    'Loading initial ramdisk ...'
    initrd    /boot/initrd.img-2.6.35-24-generic
}
### END /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###
### END /etc/grub.d/20_linux_xen ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin
}
menuentry "Memory test (memtest86+, serial console 115200)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ext2
    set root='(hd1,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 896c7bf8-d2c1-4844-aa49-95e840b77995
    linux16    /boot/memtest86+.bin console=ttyS0,115200n8
}
### END /etc/grub.d/20_memtest86+ ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###
menuentry "Windows 7 (loader) (on /dev/sda1)" {
    insmod part_msdos
    insmod ntfs
    set root='(hd0,msdos1)'
    search --no-floppy --fs-uuid --set 4eccdcc8ccdcac09
    chainloader +1
}
### END /etc/grub.d/30_os-prober ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.
### END /etc/grub.d/40_custom ###

### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###
if [ -f  $prefix/custom.cfg ]; then
  source $prefix/custom.cfg;
fi
### END /etc/grub.d/41_custom ###

```

 Create backups in case all goes wrong or you could run 

```

sudo update-grub

```

to rescan your hard drive and reset your configuration

### Post by Rubi1200 on 2011-01-21
The grub.cfg file should not be edited directly!

There is a new GUI application for customizing GRUB:
[http://www1.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10340183&postcount=1](http://www1.ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=10340183&postcount=1)

### Post by JKyleOKC on 2011-01-21
If the machine originally had Vista installed at the factory, the "Vista" that you are seeing in the grub menu is probably the hidden recovery partition that many firms put onto the drive. If you run "sudo update-grub" it will change the menu to show only things that are actually present on the drive. If you don't want them at all, the right way to get rid of them is to remove them from the drive and then run update-grub again.

To get rid of the extra versions of the Ubuntu kernel, open up Synaptic Package Manager and look them up. Mark each for removal, being careful NOT to remove your current version (and it's a good idea to save the immediately-previous version also, in case of problems with the latest one). Then click "Apply" and follow the prompts.

To get rid of the hidden Vista recovery partition, use Win 7's disk manager. Again, be certain you select the right one because once you remove the partition, you may not be able to put it back if you made a mistake!

After pruning things down, then run "sudo update-grub" again and enjoy your new, slimmer boot menu!

---

### Post by Jacobah on 2011-01-21

> If the machine originally had Vista installed at the factory, the "Vista" that you are seeing in the grub menu is probably the hidden recovery partition that many firms put onto the drive. If you run "sudo update-grub" it will change the menu to show only things that are actually present on the drive. If you don't want them at all, the right way to get rid of them is to remove them from the drive and then run update-grub again.

To get rid of the extra versions of the Ubuntu kernel, open up Synaptic Package Manager and look them up. Mark each for removal, being careful NOT to remove your current version (and it's a good idea to save the immediately-previous version also, in case of problems with the latest one). Then click "Apply" and follow the prompts.

To get rid of the hidden Vista recovery partition, use Win 7's disk manager. Again, be certain you select the right one because once you remove the partition, you may not be able to put it back if you made a mistake!

After pruning things down, then run "sudo update-grub" again and enjoy your new, slimmer boot menu!
I've actually completely wiped the drive with win7 previously and it's still there. Very odd...

### Post by JKyleOKC on 2011-01-21
Perhaps Win7 is honoring the "hidden" attribute; have you examined the disk with Ubuntu's partition management tool "gparted"? Normally it's best to use Windows tools to deal with Windows partitions, but if they won't work...

As a last resort you can edit the grub.cfg file despite the warnings not to do so, but your changes will vanish the next time grub or the kernel gets updated and update-grub runs again.

---

### Post by Quackers on 2011-01-21
Please go to the site below and download the boot script to your DESKTOP and then open up a terminal (Applications > Accessories > terminal) and run
```

sudo bash ~/Desktop/boot_info_script*.sh
```

This will produce a results.txt file on your desktop. Please copy the contents of that file and paste them in your next post between CODE tags. For CODE tags click on New Reply (not quick reply)and then click on the # symbol in the toolbar.
This will give a full overview of your current system.
Thanks.

[http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/](http://bootinfoscript.sourceforge.net/)

---
